input.query_abstract: We review theoretical approaches to the understanding of food webs. After an overview of the available food web data, we discuss three different classes of models. The first class comprise static models, which assign links between species according to some simple rule. The second class are dynamical models, which include the population dynamics of several interacting species. We focus on the question of the stability of such webs. The third class are species assembly models and evolutionary models, which build webs starting from a few species by adding new species through a process of "invasion" (assembly models) or "speciation" (evolutionary models). Evolutionary models are found to be capable of building large stable webs.
input.hyp_abstract: We review models of software dependency ecosystems, where packages are nodes joined by directed 'requires' relationships. Beginning with an overview of empirical dependency graphs mined from public package registries, we group modeling efforts into three families. The first family consists of static generators, which attach dependency edges between components using simple probabilistic attachment rules tuned to reproduce observed in- and out-degree distributions and reuse frequencies. The second family consists of dynamical models, in which the load, version churn, or fault state of mutually dependent components co-evolves under coupled update equations; we focus on stability, asking when a healthy operating point of the ecosystem resists propagating breakage, and answer it by linearizing the coupled dynamics and inspecting the leading eigenvalue of the interaction matrix. We note that more densely coupled ecosystems tend toward fragility, so that heavy reuse trades functionality against susceptibility to cascading regressions. The third family consists of growth models, in which the ecosystem is built incrementally: new components are published either de novo or by forking and adapting existing ones, and an adoption filter prunes packages that attract no downstream users. We demonstrate that such publish-and-select growth assembles large, long-lived dependency structures with the layering, heavy-tailed reuse, and near-decomposability observed in real repositories, and that these are more resilient to maintainer dropout than comparably sized random graphs.
input.candidate_abstract: In the last few years we have witnessed the emergence, primarily in on-line communities, of new types of social networks that require for their representation more complex graph structures than have been employed in the past. One example is the folksonomy, a tripartite structure of users, resources, and tags -- labels collaboratively applied by the users to the resources in order to impart meaningful structure on an otherwise undifferentiated database. Here we propose a mathematical model of such tripartite structures which represents them as random hypergraphs. We show that it is possible to calculate many properties of this model exactly in the limit of large network size and we compare the results against observations of a real folksonomy, that of the on-line photography web site Flickr. We show that in some cases the model matches the properties of the observed network well, while in others there are significant differences, which we find to be attributable to the practice of multiple tagging, i.e., the application by a single user of many tags to one resource, or one tag to many resources.
